list.text: https://www.desmos.com/3d/e3qg1xifqr
The drone is set to chase the randomized 'target' point, but you can change the individual variables to manually control the drone.
Start the ticker to watch it fly! Click the drone to reset the simulation.
Check out any of the folders below for an explanation of each part of the project.
Turn this slider to 1 to show some debug lines that help you look at what the drone is "thinking"
Simulation running too slow? Turn this slider to 1 to disable some rendering features and turn on "Disable Lighting" in the settings button (the wrench icon) at the top right
The P, I, and D values for the rotation controller
Errors and integrals required for the operation of the controller
The P, I, and D values for the vertical translation controller
Errors and integrals required for the operation of the controller
The P, I, and D values for the lateral translation controller
Errors and integrals required for the operation of the controller
This controller is unique in that it controls the target rotation of the drone in order to indirectly cause lateral movement, so here we are setting the target values for the rotation controller
Defining some colours for use in rendering

Here I am rendering the model of the drone, which I created with a CAD software known as Onshape. I then did some postprocessing in Blender, a regular 3D modeling software. Finally, I wrote a script that let me turn those 3D models into sets of points and faces which I can copy paste into Desmos and render using the triangle function.
This is where I'm creating the grass at the bottom of the scene. Higher values for GRASSCOUNT look pretty cool but they come at quite the performance loss!
Drawing some lines to see what the drone is "thinking" (this can be turned off by setting the 'debug' slider at the top of the graph to 0)

This is how I'm able to create the whirring sound of the propellers. I am creating a sawtooth wave using multiple harmonics of sine waves and changing the frequency and amplitude based on the thrust that each propeller is producing
These are the majority of the variables that are required for operation. Notably, 't_1' through 't_4' are the thrust values from each propeller as a number from 0 to 1.
Here you can find most of the constants that represent the many different parts of this simulation.
These are all of the actions that I'm using. The 'tick' action is what you can find in the ticker, and it calls all of the other actions that are completed each tick

list.latex: u_{pdatevars}=y_{pe}\to y_{err},x_{pe}\to x_{err},z_{pe}\to z_{err},y_{rpe}\to y_{re},x_{rpe}\to x_{re},u_{pdateerrs}

list.text: These are the helper functions that I'm using, most of the are for rotation, which is pretty complicated.

vector parts:
x: rotation about x
y: rotation about y
z: rotation about z
rotation applied in xyz order
function name:
r (rotation) of _ about _
r about _
